coredata.dc:title: Corrosion measurement of thermally sprayed carbide coatings on stainless steel pipes

coredata.dc:description: The petrochemical industries face a formidable challenge from corrosion, which leads to high maintenance costs and lost production. Traditional corrosion monitoring methods frequently fail to provide accurate and reliable results. As a result, these components must be replaced or repaired promptly to avoid increasing costs and limiting component life, which will reduce operation efficiency. Novel techniques with demands for high surface qualities in the future will be applied to develop alloys with better performance and more diverse applications. This study examines the corrosion resistance of cermet coatings, such as tungsten carbide (WC) and Chromium carbide (Cr3C2), in acidic solution by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and electrochemical potentiodynamic polarisation. Additionally, utilizing optical microscopy techniques, the microstructural characteristics of coatings were assessed. According to the results of the Tafel polarisation test, Cr3C2 coating exhibited a lower rate of corrosion than other coatings. The Cr3C2 coated, WC coated, and uncoated samples reported corrosion rates of 0.924, 1.122, and 2.599 mmpy respectively. The corrosion resistance of Cr3C2 and WC coatings was increased by 64% and 53%, respectively, in comparison to uncoated specimens. This suggests that the coating maximizes economy, minimizes maintenance costs, extends the structure's lifespan, and reduces down the rate of corrosion.

head.ce:title: Corrosion measurement of thermally sprayed carbide coatings on stainless steel pipes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The petrochemical industries face a formidable challenge from corrosion, which leads to high maintenance costs and lost production. Traditional corrosion monitoring methods frequently fail to provide accurate and reliable results. As a result, these components must be replaced or repaired promptly to avoid increasing costs and limiting component life, which will reduce operation efficiency. Novel techniques with demands for high surface qualities in the future will be applied to develop alloys with better performance and more diverse applications. This study examines the corrosion resistance of cermet coatings, such as tungsten carbide (WC) and Chromium carbide (Cr3C2), in acidic solution by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and electrochemical potentiodynamic polarisation. Additionally, utilizing optical microscopy techniques, the microstructural characteristics of coatings were assessed. According to the results of the Tafel polarisation test, Cr3C2 coating exhibited a lower rate of corrosion than other coatings. The Cr3C2 coated, WC coated, and uncoated samples reported corrosion rates of 0.924, 1.122, and 2.599 mmpy respectively. The corrosion resistance of Cr3C2 and WC coatings was increased by 64% and 53%, respectively, in comparison to uncoated specimens. This suggests that the coating maximizes economy, minimizes maintenance costs, extends the structure's lifespan, and reduces down the rate of corrosion.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The transportation of chemicals is susceptible to internal corrosion, particularly in severe offshore conditions. A wide range of elements impact the corrosion mechanism, resulting in multifaceted and hazardous environmental effects. Despite significant investments in corrosion reduction and control, the cost of corrosion-related asset failure is escalating (Dao et al., 2023). Corrosion is identified as the major cause of unanticipated pipeline system failures (Adumene et al., 2022). The intricate driving mechanisms of corrosion lead to unpredictable characteristics that make monitoring and control challenging. Research has shown that interior corrosion in pipeline failures caused significant financial losses (www.phmsa.dot.gov/dataand-statistics/pipeline/data-and-statistics-overview/, 2022). Predicting the internal corrosion rate is therefore crucial for maintaining asset integrity and ensuring that materials are transported through pipelines at a reasonable cost (Khan et al., 2021). Sand, debris, and other sediments transported with the fluid during operations accumulate and form deposits at the pipeline surfaces depending on the terrain and other operating conditions. These deposits form distinct local micro-environments that differ greatly from the transported fluid's properties (Jawwad and Mohamed, 2020). Besides these conditions, the deposits obstruct the dispersal of the corrosion products. The accumulation of metallic corrosion products has the potential to accelerate the corrosion process. These strategies rely on risk variables relating to operations and the environment. A corrosive metal pipeline experiences uniform corrosion, which erodes its intrinsic strength over its complete surface. The fluid transportation system may come to an end if a pipeline fails due to weak integral strength. As a result, consistent corrosion may result in significant loss. Temperature, pH, and water wetness, in pipelines all affect this corrosion. In addition to crevice corrosion, localized corrosion resulting from various corrosion mechanisms can also appear as pitting (Calabokis et al., 2021). Pitting is more harmful than uniform corrosion because it is harder to detect, anticipate, and prevent. Pitting in carbon steel is thought to be caused by product films breaking down on a metal surface, according to a study (HeidaryK.MGroth, 2021). The pitted holes in sweet systems are small but have sharp edges. A type of localized corrosion in a chloride-containing environment is called pitting. Small metal surface scrapes brought on by localized corrosion eventually lead to equipment or pipe perforations. Erosion has the ability to remove these layers of corrosion products from systems where the corrosion products offer some protection from corrosion, leaving the newly exposed metal surface vulnerable to corrosion (Andreini et al., 2019). The protective coating will act as a corrosion inhibitor, preventing the formation of metal oxide on the metal's surface. The assessments now available on corrosion prevention and control methods for metallic pipelines concentrate on methods for preventing certain types of corrosion, such as pitting, biofilms, hydrogen embrittlement, and top-of-the-line (TOL) corrosion (Liu et al., 2016; Ohaeri et al., 2018). Additionally, some studies exclusively address the corrosion inhibitors in metallic pipelines (Al Shibli et al., 2022). Finally, additional evaluations concentrated on certain or particular methods of protection and control such as material selection, cathodic protection (CP), inhibitors, coatings, and corrosion monitoring and inspection (Szeliga, 2012). Since the origin causes of corrosion vary, the various ways of protecting against it should be covered and explored individually. Furthermore, no review work has thoroughly discussed corrosion prevention methods for metallic pipelines. As a result, the purpose of this article is to close the knowledge gaps in the previous reviews. This comprehensive study, which covers research gaps and offers insights and future directions, appears to be an early attempt to address in-depth corrosion protection and control strategies for metallic pipelines.

One important component of corrosion protection and control methods is the choice of materials for a specific environment. The selection of material is essential for managing different types of corrosion and breaks down, even if no material is resistant to all corrosive conditions. Several factors and requirements, including physical and mechanical properties, resistance to corrosion, design availability, affordability, maintainability, compatibility with other components of the system, reliability, and life expectancy, should be achieved by the chosen material (Farh et al., 2023). The resistance of the material is affected by the corrosive environment. A substance could be extremely resistant to corrosion in one environment, but not in another (Li et al., 2016). Thus, the key to a material's effective application lies in understanding how resistant it is to corrosion in a specific environment. Pipeline material is influenced by both pipe operation conditions (Maraveas, 2002). Maintaining safety is an even more vital issue. Accordingly, industrial applications need to specify and employ materials with high mechanical strength and corrosion resistance, which has resulted in several researches (El Rayes et al., 2013). Thus, selecting materials for corrosion service poses a huge challenge, for which there are no definitive solutions in the majority of published works. The microstructure currently available in duplex stainless steels comprises roughly 50/50 ferrite and austenite, combining the toughness and flexibility of austenite with the exceptional strength of ferrite. A few advantages of introducing nitrogen are improved weldability, stronger austenite, increased corrosion resistance, and better element equip-partitioning between the two phases. (Foct et al., 1991). The duplex stainless steels are widely used as resistant to corrosion in a variety of conditions. They also have good corrosion resistance due to their great strength and hardness (Francis and Byrne, 2021). Many of the duplex compositions are also available in cast form, and they have been widely used for equipment such as pumps and valves (Francis, 2020). Therefore, these alloys are easily obtainable in a variety of different forms with an extremely appealing array of features. Numerous industries have used duplex stainless steel to varied degrees. The chemical processing, pulp and paper, power, petroleum and marine sectors all make extensive use of them. Due to their excellent resistance to atmospheric corrosion, DSS have replaced carbon steel in a variety of applications since they require no upkeep or painting in harsh environments (Francis, 2018). Ramkumar et al. established that exceptional mechanical characteristics and a substantial level of erosion-corrosion resistance may be obtained in DSS with equal amounts of austenite and ferrite phases (Ramkumar et al., 2016). DSS 2205 is suitable for industries that are prone to erosion and corrosion (Renner et al., 2021). Due to its higher mechanical strength and resistance to corrosion, DSS 2205 is recommended over conventional austenitic stainless steels. Compared to other materials, DSS offers some budgetary benefits, including guaranteed corrosion resistance and cheaper alloy costs (Conradi et al., 2011). The most often used DSS grade, according to Xiao et al., is DSS, which includes chromium, nickel, molybdenum, and additional alloying elements like silicon, nitrogen, and manganese (Xiao et al., 2021). Because of its remarkable corrosion resistance, DSS material is a good option for use in marine environment (Kuruvila et al., 2023).

To meet the functional demands of applications related to the petrochemical industries, customized alloy chemistry is often developed in response to the degradation of engineering components, particularly while operating in relatively severe environments that are prone to corrosion. Nonetheless, the total expense of developing novel alloys and the requirement for specialized manufacturing processes can limit the usefulness of such cutting-edge materials. Alternatively, a novel technique that is heavily utilized by modern businesses is the deposition of coatings with such an advanced material composition on components built from ordinary alloys. To meet these demands, several coating deposition processes have been developed (Meghwal et al., 2020). One adaptable and quickly developing surface modification technique is thermal spray coating (Kuruvila et al., 2022a). The most adaptable thermal spray method with minimal restrictions on the materials that can be sprayed is atmospheric plasma spraying (APS) (Łatka et al., 2020). A feedstock is fed into the plasma stream and allowed to melt completely or partially, finally depositing onto the substrate as lamellar splats. Coating density, quality, and uniformity are determined by a number of process parameters, the most important of which are gas flow rate, power input, carrier gas flow rate, stand-off distance, and powder feed rate. Although it can be adjusted to minimize metal powder oxidation, APS is most effective at generating ceramic coatings with porosities of less than a small percentage (Kuruvila et al., 2022b). Advances in feedstock materials for thermal spray technology have been observed in metal alloys, composites, intermetallics, ceramics, and cermets; the specific selection is contingent upon the ultimate use. For the production of high-quality coatings, the feedstock of the highest caliber in terms of particle morphology and particle size distribution—also known as the "cut"—is essential. Materials research has been driven towards the creation of novel alloys due to the concurrent rise in demanding industrial environments and slow progress in conventional materials (Berger, 2015; Kuruvila et al., 2021, 2024).

Compared to other materials, carbide-based materials offer superior resistance to wear, erosion, and corrosion. To improve surface qualities, materials based on carbide can be applied to a variety of surfaces. Carbide coatings significantly reduce component maintenance costs while extending the life of coated components. Applications for carbide-based coatings are numerous, including boiler and turbine components, aerospace and maritime uses, and the energy sector. The two main carbide materials utilized for wear resistance and corrosion resistance are tungsten carbide (WC) and chromium carbide (Cr3C2). WC coatings provide excellent wear resistance and bonding strength. Cr3C2 coatings have the potential to be a great substitute for tungsten carbide in high-temperature applications depending on the specific working conditions. These coatings provide high-temperature resistance for components in the power plant environment (Govande et al., 2022). The application of carbide-based cermet coatings for corrosion resistance has been the subject of numerous investigations (Babu et al., 2018). To attain the best possible combination of wear and corrosion resistance, efforts have continuously been made to improve the quality of thermal spray carbide-based cermet coatings. The most researched and used cermet systems are coatings based on WC and Cr3C2, because of their combined high toughness and hardness (Zhang et al., 2019). Carbides resist wear, and the dispersion of these ceramic elements inside a malleable metallic matrix ensures resistance against impact. Numerous authors conclude that coatings based on WC and Cr3C2 can minimize material loss by enhancing their surface qualities (Varis et al., 2020). The majority of these studies concentrate on the way that metals corrode in acidic environments. To the best of our knowledge, no published research has examined these materials' resistance to corrosion in seawater (Wang et al., 2013). This constitutes a significant gap in the literature as these ceramic materials are frequently used as pipeline coatings and understanding their corrosion is essential for both appropriate coating selection and lifespan estimation.

Assessing the corrosion rate of coated specimens is crucial from a technological perspective for estimating their lifetime and comparing their ability to protect against corrosion. Several methods for determining the rate of corrosion such as weight loss, hydrogen evolution, and polarisation curves are commonly employed. Electrochemical techniques are appropriate when they follow the two key features. Firstly, approaches must be primarily simple and reliable, especially when used in the context of continuous corrosion observation. Secondly, non-destructive methods are required to minimize the harmful effect on the materials being inspected. Therefore, to more precisely monitor the corrosion, it would be excellent to use an electrochemical technique (Xia et al., 2022). Song (2005), highlighted the point that while creating this kind of material, design engineers and corrosion specialists must consider the corrosion rate as a crucial factor. From a technological perspective, establishing trustworthy corrosion rate values is a crucial first step in estimating the service life of structures made of magnesium alloys, comparing the durability of various alloys, and managing the efficacy of protective measures. As noted by Lorenz et al., practical applications greatly benefit from having quick and precise techniques for measuring corrosion rates (Lorenz and Mansfeld, 1981). Numerous studies have extensively employed electrochemical methods to examine the corrosion behavior of coating materials (Rouhi et al., 2011).

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) methodology is an innovative approach to corrosion rate monitoring. Impedance methods are used in this technique. The main benefit of employing the EIS method is that, in contrast with non-electrochemical techniques, low system corrosion has no effect on measurement accuracy. Due to the electrochemical characteristics inherent in the corrosion process (Husairi et al., 2014). EIS is an essential technique for monitoring electrochemical changes in a system as it offers kinetic and mechanistic information on the corrosion process in the case of passive and active protection, or a combination of both. (Aparicio and Mosa, 2018). It is essential for the very reason that it provides measurable information about how the system functions (Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2014). After examining electrochemical corrosion behavior, metallurgical characterization was carried out for both coated specimens using scanning electron microscopy.

An extensive analysis of the corrosion mechanisms of carbide coatings applied thermally and deposited on steel substrates is provided in this study. The investigation, assessment, and comparison of several thermal-sprayed coating materials and their corrosion mechanisms in acidic media was conducted. Based on the prior study in this field, relevant areas and knowledge gaps that require further investigation are thus identified. The primary objective of this article is to provide a thorough overview of the developments in corrosion protection in acidic environments, with an emphasis on surface treatment methods. The basic elements of corrosion in industry, conventional and innovative corrosion protection techniques, the role of developing technologies, and the regulatory and environmental variables influencing these advancements are all discussed in this study. The fact that most of these studies concentrate on the corrosion behavior of metals in alkaline conditions presents another issue with corrosion. To the best of our knowledge, no published research has examined these materials' corrosion resistance in acidic environments, up to this point. This is a significant gap in the literature because these ceramic materials are frequently used as pipeline coatings. Understanding their corrosion rates is essential for appropriate coating selection and lifespan predictions (Luiz et al., 2021).

The objective of this research is to determine whether WC and Cr3C2 coatings on duplex stainless steel, continue to be appropriate for industrial applications and to assess the corrosion resistance of these coatings. Furthermore, to address the challenges and prospects in this field while highlighting the industry's corrosion resistance advancements. The industry would benefit economically if comparable corrosion resistance could be achieved along with the successful completion of the aforementioned goals.

Duplex stainless steel (DSS) 2205 [Fe = 69.85%, Cr = 24.45%, Ni = 3.95%, Mn = 0.85%, Si = 0.78%, P = 0.02%, S = 0.03%, C = 0.02%], a material that is commonly used, has been selected as the substrate material for coating. The material was cut by a water jet abrasive cutting machine to dimensions of 25 mm × 25 mm × 5 mm to be used as substrate material. To get rid of any remaining bits of grit, the specimens were grit-blasted before deposition.

The suggested method for deposition against corrosion is thermal spraying. Coatings with lower porosity, higher hardness, and extremely high bonding strengths can be produced using the atmospheric plasma method. Various coatings have been successfully produced on substrates using the plasma spraying method (Goudarzi et al., 2022). As a result, the procedures that are frequently employed in industries are those that use plasma spraying.

Owing to their combined high hardness and toughness, tungsten carbide and chromium carbide coatings are the most researched and used cermet systems (Alam and Das, 2022). In industrial environments, WC and Cr3C2 coatings are frequently utilized to prevent corrosion (Somasundaram et al., 2022). In this study, the two cermet powders which are used for this study are WC and Cr3C2 (supplied by Spraymet, Bengaluru). The typical form and the elemental composition of the WC and Cr3C2 powders as received are shown in Figs. 1 and 2
respectively. APS is the thermal spraying method that is accepted for corrosion-resistant coatings. The depositional parameters for both WC and Cr3C2 coatings are listed in Table 1 . Before coatings were used in industrial settings, their microstructure and hardness were evaluated. Table 2 shows the physical properties of both WC and Cr3C2.

Defining the spray conditions is the initial stage of the experiment's design. Previous research that has been published has validated the identification of the significant components that have a greater influence on the coating (Koivuluoto et al., 2020). Excellent hardness, low porosity, and a lower surface roughness are desired in coatings. It has been proposed to optimize input parameters using Taguchi-based Grey relational analysis since traditional methods of performing multidimensional problems are time-consuming. With these goals in consideration, Taguchi's L9 orthogonal array has been implemented using the Minitab 18® statistical tool. Table 3 shows the three input factors, with three levels. While choosing the levels, a larger range of input values was taken into consideration in order to produce better outcomes.

The Grey relational grade is generated by assigning equal weights to the Grey relational coefficient of each individual response in multi response optimization, which takes into account of all of the output responses individually. The deviation sequence was followed by the determination of the Grey relationship coefficient and grade for every individual response. Every response level value is formulated based on the computed Grey relational grade. The response table and main effects plot calculations indicated the ideal coating condition.

The corrosion behavior of the coated specimens was studied by the electrochemical techniques like Open Circuit Potential Monitoring (OCP), EIS and potentiodynamic polarisation (PDP) in 1.0 wt% H2SO4 corrosive electrolyte solution using an electrochemical system with a computer controlled potentiostat (Zive SP1, WonATech Co., Ltd. Korea). A flat three electrode electrochemical cell with a Ag/AgCl reference electrode, a graphite auxiliary electrode and test specimens as working electrode was used for the electrochemical measurements. All the electrochemical analyses were carried out at room temperature (27 °C). The area of the specimen exposed to the electrolyte was 1 cm2.

The OCP was monitored for 30 min in accordance with ASTM C876 after exposing the specimen to the electrolyte for equilibration. The EIS measurements were performed at the OCP with a sinusoidal AC signal in the frequency range of 10 mHz–100 kHz at an amplitude of ±10 mV and the resulting impedance values are displayed as Nyquist plots, Bode plots and phase angle plots. The specimens were polarized in cathodic and anodic directions within a potential window of ±350 mV with respect to OCP and potentiodynamic polarisation study was carried out at a scan rate of 0.166 mV/s. By extrapolating the linear portion of the curve, corrosion current density (Icorr), corrosion potential (Ecorr), polarisation resistance (Rp) and corrosion rate (CR) were determined. After being repeated three times, the average value from each experiment has been taken for analysis.
Taguchi's Design of Experiments was used to conduct the experiments. The measured values for surface roughness, porosity, and hardness for the coated specimens are shown in Table 4 .

The cross-sectional views of the samples with WC and Cr3C2 coatings are provided in Fig. 4 . The SEM (Joel, model JSM-IT510) was employed to assess the surface morphology of the coated surfaces of the sprayed samples. Images exhibit exceptional bonds at the edge and no evidence of debonding. All of these coatings have an average coating thickness of about 100 μm and a microhardness of 1096 Hv for WC and 949 Hv for Cr3C2 coated samples. The microhardness was determined with a 500 g load and a 20 s dwell time using a micro Vickers hardness tester. 10 measurements were taken on average across a 20 mm length. The average value was used for the analysis (Mazaheri et al., 2022).

The porosity of sprayed coatings was evaluated using the ASTM E2109-01 standard. The measurements were performed under ambient conditions. Coatings were evaluated using the guidelines provided by prior publications (Maruszczyk et al., 2017). Both samples' computed porosity values were in line with those of plasma coatings. The values for Uncoated, WC and Cr3C2 were 6.9%, 3.8% and 2.4%, respectively. The WC powder's high melting point (about 3143 K) could be the reason for the WC coating's increased porosity (Jonda et al., 2021).

A stylus profilometer (Handy Surf Surface profilometer, Handy Surf 35 A) was used to measure the surface roughness of sprayed coatings. As the average of five measurements, the results are 6.7 μm, 3.8 μm and 2.9 μm, for Uncoated, WC and Cr3C2 coatings respectively. When compared to the CrC coating, it is evident that all of the WC coating's surface topographical metrics are higher. The same procedure was carried out to measure the surface roughness after corrosion. Both coatings had higher surface roughness values; the Uncoated, WC and Cr3C2 coatings have values of 9.2 μm, 4.25 μm and 3.21 μm, respectively. As corrosion occurs, the surface roughness increases as a result of the products of corrosion forming on the surface. This indicates that the surfaces are producing deeper grooves on rougher surfaces and a passive layer on smoother surfaces (Toloei et al., 2015).

All of the specimens had good adhesion between the dense coating layer and substrate, according to the cross-sectional micrographs. The rapid solidification and severe plastic deformation produced by the high-velocity interaction of semi-molten coating materials on the substrate means that thermal spray coating layers are likely to contain unavoidable pores and micro cracks (De Villiers Lovelock et al., 1998). Uncoated, WC, and Cr3C2 exhibited the highest microhardness and porosity. The decarburization of WC coating powders during the coating process is responsible for enhancing cracks in WC coating (Stewart et al., 2000). As a result, it was concluded that the higher corrosion in the coating layer was triggered by the solution's infiltration into the coating layer/substrate interface through the micro cracks. Simard et al. (2000) also observed and reported a similar conclusion. Fig. 5 exhibits SEM images of the surface for WC, and Cr3C2 samples. The microstructure of uncoated specimens is distinctive, showing obvious pores, cavities, etc.; in contrast, the microstructure of Cr3C2 and WC coatings are thick and consistent, with excellent cross adhesion.

The electrochemical polarisation method has been used to evaluate the corrosion behaviors of DSS2205 stainless steels with coated and uncoated. The polarisation curves are experimentally plotted for all the specimens at room temperature to determine the corrosion behavior and the components of corrosion such as Ecorr, Icorr and corrosion rate (CR). Anodic current, which results from the oxidation process, and cathodic current that results from the reduction reaction, begin to flow due to the controlled electrochemical reaction (resulting from the reduction reaction).

The TAFEL extrapolation method was used to determine the corresponding Ecorr and Icorr. Icorr is a measurement of how quickly a material corrodes, whereas Ecorr is a measure of how easily metals corrode (Chen et al., 2019). Therefore, corrosion kinetics indicates that when corrosion current density declines, the test material's corrosion resistance improves (Miranda et al., 2006). In comparison to other coated and uncoated specimens, Cr3C2 coated specimens have greater Ecorr values and lower Icorr values. This implies that there is a lower propensity for the Cr3C2 coating to corrode. In contrast to WC coatings, Cr3C2 coatings have exhibited much lower rates of corrosion and degradation.

When the potential scan is complete, you can plot current on a log scale on the y-axis and potential on the x-axis to generate a Tafel plot. There should be regions in the Tafel plot where the log of current varies linearly with potential on both the cathodic slope and the anodic slope. Once a linear fit is done on both of these regions, these fitted linear lines should be projected to where they intersect. The intersection's potential value (x-axis reading) and current value (y-axis), respectively, are used to calculate corrosion potential and current.

The potentiodynamic polarisation curves of WC, Cr3C2 coated, and uncoated DSS2205 are displayed in Fig. 6 . The critical variables obtained by fitting polarisation curves are shown in Table 7 . The uncoated specimens, WC, and Cr3C2 possess distinct Ecorr values. The Cr3C2 coating had the highest Ecorr value (−0.2429) while for WC and uncoated were −0.2424 and −0.2418 V, respectively. An increase in Cr was beneficial to Cr3C2. The specimens' CR and Icorr results showed a similar trend. A substantial impact of the increase in Cr % was seen in the values of Icorr and CR. In general, a greater Ecorr indicates better chemical stability and a decreased tendency to corrode, while a lower Icorr denotes a reduction in rate of corrosion (Yang et al., 2020). In addition, it can be observed that in the anode polarisation area from 0 to 1 V, the corrosion current density of WC is significantly higher than that of WC and uncoated. The Cr3C2 coating has superior corrosion stability when compared to WC coatings and uncoated. Although the current density is constant over a specific potential range, the curves do not exhibit active-passive behavior, and even at reasonably high potentials, no breakdown potential can be seen. According to the findings, Cr3C2 coating reduces anodic and cathodic current density and results in Ecorr shift in a less negative way. Smaller values of Icorr and higher levels of Rp in Cr3C2 coatings than in WC coatings indicated an improvement in the corrosion performance. Inadequate passivation areas can be seen on the curves, indicating that the alloys tend to passivate. In comparison to the uncoated samples, the passivation regions expanded as the WC % increased. This might be due to WC particles, which are made of ceramic and have a protective surface, with high corrosion resistance. The increased presence of chromium carbides increases the corrosion resistance. It is the primary cause of the rise in the passivation range for Cr3C2 coating. The formation of Cr-rich carbide phases and the presence of WC particles with a homogenous dispersal in the matrix enhance corrosion resistance. The addition of WC improves the surface's passivation and shields the internal layer from contact with the corrosive media, both of which might reduce the corrosion reaction. The development of W-rich and Cr-rich carbides may be responsible for the improvement in passivation (Khallaf et al., 2022).

Though the specimens under examination had varying degrees of corrosion resistance, Cr3C2 coating demonstrated the best overall performance. Very little degradation was evident when the coating was in an acidic environment. The chromium content of the Cr-rich carbides, which have been proven to be crucial for corrosion resistance, are responsible for its exceptional corrosion resistance. Uncoated specimens suffered the worst and displayed the most surface damage in electrochemical testing. The coatings' tendency to corrode can also be attributed to the reinforcing particles present in it. In the present study examining the corrosion behavior of coatings based on WC and Cr3C2, coatings based on Cr3C2 outperformed coatings based on WC in terms of resistance to corrosive attack.

The equivalent electric circuits displayed in Fig. 7 were used to develop methods that improve their impedance analysis while offering quantitative evidence for the experimental results of EIS. Table 8 provides the numerical results of the experimental EIS data fitting in the 1% H2SO4 solution. A high association exists between variations of matching Icorr values obtained using polarisation curves and the results of the EIS. Since a layer's resistivity and corrosion resistance are strongly correlated, the fitted values of the EIS plots indicate that the samples coated with Cr3C2 are more corrosion-resistant than the samples that are uncoated and coated with WC.

Fig. 8 exhibits the OCP of the uncoated, WC, and Cr3C2. Compared to WC and uncoated, the OCP of Cr3C2 coating demonstrates superior characteristics. A higher OCP indicates a more stable passivation layer and a lower propensity for corrosion (Katiyar and Randhawa, 2019).

EIS measurements were performed to determine the susceptibility of the coatings to corrosion. Fig. 9 provides the EIS charts for the WC, Cr3C2, and uncoated. The EIS shows an identically deformed semicircle in the Nyquist plot, confirming the consistency of their failure modes. The deformed semicircle's radius is Cr3C2 > WC > uncoated. The deformed semicircle's higher diameter shows that it has outstanding barrier characteristics (Liu et al., 2017). The sequence of the impedance is Cr3C2 >WC > uncoated as observed from the Bode impedance plot. The Bode impedance plot's lowest frequency impedance modulus (|Z|0.01 Hz) is utilized as a semi-quantitative measure of the coating's corrosion resistance (Saha and Khan, 2010). According to the indications, the order of the electron transfer rate is Cr3C2 < WC < uncoated. Following are comparisons of corrosion rates: Cr3C2 < WC < uncoated. Thus, Cr3C2 exhibits the maximum degree of corrosion resistance, which is in line with what the dynamic polarisation curves predicted. This is followed by WC and uncoated. In the same condition, it becomes obvious that Cr3C2 anti-corrosion diameter is larger than that of the WC. Additionally, the Cr3C2 electron transfer rate is lower than that of WC and uncoated specimens. In comparison to WC, the Cr3C2 corrodes very slowly. The solution's permeability influences its resistance to corrosion. The Cr3C2 coatings offer superior corrosion resistance than the WC coatings since they are less porous and have a more homogeneous structure, which effectively prevents fluid from permeating the coating and improves corrosion resistance (Zhou et al., 2020).

Solution resistance is represented by Rs, Rp, coating capacitance, coating resistance, and double-layer capacitance respectively. Rp is a reliable measure of a coating's resistance to penetration and is frequently employed to evaluate its efficacy. Further, as the two coatings differ chemically, they exhibit diverse corrosion behavior and have different effects on the electrochemical impedance spectrum. However, compared to a bare specimen, the coating exhibits very different behavior. Coating corrosion generally occurs through common paths such as fissures, pores with connections, interlamellar or unmelted particle borders (Agüero et al., 2011). The high Cr concentration and lack of flaws in the Cr3C2 coating samples make them exceptional shields for the stainless-steel substrates. In contrast, the WC-coated and uncoated samples' imperfections such as micro cracks and porosity, inadequately isolate the substrate's surface from the corrosive media, allowing corrosion to occur.

The surface morphology of the coatings following polarisation tests in H2SO4 solution was studied by SEM to better understand the differences in the corrosion resistance of the coatings. The degree of corrosion is determined by the presence of surface voids and fissures (Saha and Khan, 2010). All samples experienced coating detachment after polarisation tests. Pits show potential pathways for the solution to reach the substrate through the structure around flaws, causing the coating and the substrate to disintegrate. Fig. 10 shows SEM images of samples that had undergone identical processes of corrosion. There are many tiny pits and products of corrosion on the surfaces. Additionally, it was discovered that the surfaces exhibited free corrosion products along the pores. Additionally, the corrosion craters were joined to one another to develop a shape like a groove all through the corrosion process. It is obvious that the passivation film was peeling away from the surface and that an outer coating of corrosion products film had developed. When Cr3C2 and WC coatings are compared, it can be observed that the Cr3C2 coating's surface was flat, had few cracks, and got relatively dense corrosion products and passivation film. When examining the uncoated specimen, it possessed a rougher surface, deeper cracks with relatively more corrosion products.

The shape and distribution of the carbide in the Cr3C2 coating, exhibit the least material loss. The lower dissolving of the carbide results in better carbide dispersion, which improves protection. Higher oxygen levels lead to an increased Cr3C2 concentration. Despite the material's lamellar structure, this continuous and homogeneous layer of Cr3C2 improves its corrosion resistance, leading to a lower percentage of material being removed throughout the corrosion process (Espallargas et al., 2008).

The main sporadic corrosion can occur on both coated and uncoated surfaces; however, it is commonly referred to as general corrosion. Yet, from a macroscopic perspective, corrosion processes are either localized or generalized, and they begin in the nano- and micrometer-sized range. Acquisition of data on such scales is therefore necessary for a thorough understanding of the general characteristics of corrosion.

The ideal polarisation start time is difficult to determine and likely depends on the environment and alloy as it is unknown how much corrosion occurs on the surface until it becomes relatively stable.

As electrochemical tests are instantaneous, they only record an instant of time in which the corrosion is occurring. Comparing the temperature recorded at a particular moment in time with the daily average is analogous to this.

The characterization of protective coatings by electrochemical techniques indicates that the development of high-performance coatings requires both electrochemical analysis and in-depth structural information. Developing a successful barrier with the required properties such as chemical, mechanical, and thermal stability and desired properties requires an interdisciplinary approach. For both theoretical and experimental investigations, corrosion prediction is still challenging. The development of useful and effective instruments for corrosion investigations is the focus of current researches that can be used with the majority of systems that are susceptible to corrosion, among others, still need to be combined even if the field has achieved tremendous advances. High-efficiency, low-complexity multifunctional green materials are the subject of theoretical models, cutting-edge technologies, and promising future developments in coatings. The creation of eco-friendly coatings is in complete accordance with the circular economy's principles. The information on theoretical and experimental knowledge about composition, structure, and electrochemical properties obtained will be useful in developing new coatings that use eco-friendly components to provide longer-lasting safety.

Industries employ cermet coatings to mitigate the effects of corrosion-induced degradation. Nevertheless, after brief operation times, those qualities may disappear if they are not resistant to corrosion. Comparing the corrosion resistance of C3C2 and WC thermally sprayed coatings was the primary objective of this investigation. The main findings drawn from the data are as follows.
●
The ceramic coatings exhibited superior adhesion to the substrate with lower porosity, indicating that the plasma spray technique was an effective tool to produce corrosion resistant coatings.
●
Coatings based on WC and Cr3C2 showed significantly lower corrosion than uncoated stainless steel, which makes them more desirable choices for coating materials. The corrosion rate was lowered by 53% with WC coating and 64% for Cr3C2 coating.
●
Electrochemical tests proved that Cr3C2 coatings outperformed WC in terms of corrosion resistance in highly corrosive environments, making this chromium carbide coating an acceptable replacement.
●
The hardness and structural integrity of the coating are essential components in corrosion mitigation. Cr3C2 coating offers a lower material loss than WC due to its higher adherence and homogeneity.
●
Thus, Cr3C2 is proved to be the most suitable corrosion-resistant material which is used as a protective liner in acidic environments.
